walkthrough.title: Set Up the Wavelength Device
walkthrough.text: Let's get Wavelength ready! Place the Wavelength device in its tray in the center of the table. The device has a spinning wheel with a hidden target, a blue screen that hides the target, and a red dial that players will turn. Make sure the device is positioned so the active player can open and close the screen privately.

walkthrough.title: Form Two Teams
walkthrough.text: Split your group into two roughly equal teams. You can call yourselves Left Brain and Right Brain or make up your own team names. Each team picks one player to be the first Psychic, which is the clue-giving role. Teams will alternate being the active team each round.

walkthrough.title: Set the Score Tokens
walkthrough.text: Insert both team head tokens into the score tracks on the device. The team going first starts at zero points. The team going second starts at one point. This small head start balances the advantage of acting first. Place the Left/Right guess token in its slot as well.

walkthrough.title: Place the Card Deck and Begin
walkthrough.text: Keep the deck of Wavelength spectrum cards near the device as a draw pile. Each card has two sides with different spectrums. The Psychic will draw one card per round and choose which side to use. You are all set! The first team's Psychic closes the screen to start round one.

summary.title: Set Up the Wavelength Device
bullets: Place device in tray — wheel, hidden target, blue screen, red dial
Active Psychic must be able to open/close screen privately

summary.title: Form Two Teams
bullets: Split into 2 roughly equal teams
Each team picks 1 first Psychic (clue giver)

summary.title: Set the Score Tokens
bullets: First team starts at 0 pts, second team starts at 1 pt
Insert both scoring tokens + Left/Right guess token

summary.title: Place the Card Deck and Begin
bullets: Spectrum cards near device as draw pile
First Psychic closes screen → round 1 begins

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Wavelength is a team communication game where one player gives a single clue to help their teammates find a hidden target on a spectrum. The spectrum has two opposite concepts at each end, like Hot and Cold. The target is randomly placed somewhere on that spectrum. Your clue should communicate where the target lands. The closer your team sets the dial to the target, the more points you score.

walkthrough.title: Round Structure: Four Phases
walkthrough.text: Each round has four phases. First, the Psychic Phase where the active Psychic sees the hidden target and gives one clue. Second, the Team Phase where the Psychic's teammates discuss and set the dial. Third, the Left/Right Phase where the opposing team guesses whether the target is left or right of the dial. Fourth, the Scoring Phase where the target is revealed and points are awarded.

walkthrough.title: Psychic Phase: Drawing and Seeing the Target
walkthrough.text: The Psychic closes the blue screen completely, then draws one Wavelength card and chooses one side. The card shows two opposite concepts at each end, like Underrated and Overrated. The Psychic spins the wheel to randomize the hidden target, then opens the screen fully to see where the target is positioned on the spectrum. Only the Psychic sees this.

walkthrough.title: Psychic Phase: Giving a Clue
walkthrough.text: The Psychic gives exactly one clue that communicates where the target sits on the spectrum. For example, if the spectrum is Cold to Hot and the target is near the Hot end, the Psychic might say lava. If the target is near the middle, they might say room temperature. After giving the clue, the Psychic closes the screen and cannot speak, gesture, or react in any way.

walkthrough.title: Team Phase: Discussion and Dial Setting
walkthrough.text: The Psychic's teammates now discuss the clue and decide where to set the red dial on the spectrum. Any teammate can turn the dial. Debate openly about what the clue means and where on the spectrum it falls. When the team agrees, they lock their position and announce they are done. The Psychic watches in silence and cannot help.

walkthrough.title: Left/Right Phase: Opposing Team Guesses
walkthrough.text: The opposing team now makes a simple binary guess: is the actual target to the left or to the right of where the dial was placed? They discuss briefly and place the guess token on Left or Right. If they guess correctly, they earn one bonus point. This gives the non-active team something to do every round and a chance to score.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Phase: Reveal and Points
walkthrough.text: Open the screen to reveal the hidden target. The target area has three scoring zones. The center zone scores four points. The zones on either side score three and two points respectively. If the dial is completely outside the target area, the active team scores zero. The opposing team scores one point if their Left/Right guess was correct, unless the active team hit the four-point center, in which case the opposing team scores nothing.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: After scoring, the other team becomes the active team for the next round. The game ends when any team reaches ten points. The team with the higher score wins. If there is a tie, play sudden-death rounds where each team takes one turn and the team that scores more in that round wins. Repeat sudden-death rounds until the tie is broken.

summary.title: Game Overview
bullets: 1 Psychic gives 1 clue → team sets dial on a spectrum
Closer to hidden target = more points

summary.title: Round Structure: Four Phases
bullets: 1. Psychic Phase (see target, give clue)
2. Team Phase (discuss, set dial)
3. Left/Right Phase (opponents guess)
4. Scoring Phase (reveal target, award points)

summary.title: Psychic Phase: Drawing and Seeing the Target
bullets: Close screen → draw card → choose a side → spin wheel
Open screen to see target position — only Psychic sees this

summary.title: Psychic Phase: Giving a Clue
bullets: Give exactly 1 clue that communicates target position
Close screen after clue → total silence from Psychic

summary.title: Team Phase: Discussion and Dial Setting
bullets: Teammates discuss and set the red dial on the spectrum
Psychic watches silently — no help allowed

summary.title: Left/Right Phase: Opposing Team Guesses
bullets: Opposing team guesses: target is LEFT or RIGHT of the dial
Correct = 1 bonus point for opposing team

summary.title: Scoring Phase: Reveal and Points
bullets: Target zones: center = 4 pts, adjacent = 3 or 2 pts, miss = 0
Left/Right bonus = 1 pt (0 if active team hits center)
Edge tie → active team gets the better score

summary.title: Winning the Game
bullets: First team to 10 pts wins
Ties → sudden-death rounds until 1 team scores more

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Wavelength
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Wavelength! We are going to walk through two full rounds with six players divided into two teams of three. Team Sun has Alex, Dana, and Frankie. Team Moon has Blair, Casey, and Jordan. Alex is Team Sun's first Psychic. Follow along even if your group has a different number of players.

walkthrough.title: Round One: Alex Draws a Card
walkthrough.text: Alex closes the blue screen, draws a Wavelength card, and chooses the side that reads Bad Movie to Good Movie as the spectrum. Alex spins the wheel to randomize the target, then opens the screen. The target is positioned about three quarters of the way toward Good Movie. Alex needs one clue that says very good but not the absolute best.

walkthrough.title: Alex Gives a Clue
walkthrough.text: Alex thinks for a moment and says The Dark Knight. Alex chose a movie that most people consider excellent but maybe not the greatest film ever made. It signals very good without being at the absolute extreme. Alex closes the screen and goes silent. Now it is up to Dana and Frankie to figure out where on the spectrum Alex means.

walkthrough.title: Team Sun Discusses
walkthrough.text: Dana says The Dark Knight is a great movie, so it should be pretty far toward Good Movie. Frankie agrees but wonders how far. Is it at the very end or more like seventy-five percent? They debate whether Alex considers it the best ever or just really good. Dana turns the dial to about eighty percent toward Good Movie. Frankie nudges it slightly left. They lock it in.

walkthrough.title: Team Moon Makes Their Left/Right Guess
walkthrough.text: Team Moon thinks about it. The Dark Knight is great but not a universally agreed-upon best film of all time. They think the target might be slightly left of where Team Sun placed the dial. They place the guess token on Left. Now it is time to reveal the target.

walkthrough.title: The Reveal
walkthrough.text: Alex opens the screen. The target is right in the three-point zone, and the dial is touching the edge between three and four points! Team Sun scores three points. Team Moon's Left guess was wrong because the target was actually slightly right of the dial. Team Moon scores zero. A great round for Team Sun!

walkthrough.title: Mistake One: Multi-Part Clue
walkthrough.text: Round two begins. Blair is Team Moon's Psychic. The spectrum is Unethical to Ethical. The target is near the middle, leaning slightly toward Ethical. Blair says recycling but also like not perfect recycling because sometimes you mess up. That is not one clue. That is a rambling explanation that confuses the team instead of guiding them.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Give One Clean Clue
walkthrough.text: The rules say to give one clue thought. Do not combine multiple ideas, add qualifiers, or ramble. A single clean concept works much better than a multi-part explanation. For this spectrum and target, Blair could have simply said jaywalking, which most people consider mildly unethical but mostly fine, placing it just past the middle toward Ethical. Short, concrete clues are always better than long, qualified ones.

walkthrough.title: Mistake Two: Psychic Reacts to Discussion
walkthrough.text: After Blair gives the clue, Casey and Jordan start debating. Casey suggests the dial should go pretty far toward Ethical. Jordan disagrees. Blair cannot help but wince and slightly shake their head. Casey notices and says wait, Blair made a face, it must be more toward the middle! Blair just accidentally influenced the discussion through body language.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Psychic Must Stay Silent and Neutral
walkthrough.text: After giving your clue, you must become a statue. No talking, no gestures, no facial reactions. Your teammates will debate and possibly get it wrong, and you have to let it happen. Maintaining a neutral face is part of the skill of being a good Psychic. Practice keeping a poker face regardless of where your team moves the dial.

walkthrough.title: Mistake Three: Extreme Anchoring
walkthrough.text: In a later round, the spectrum is Bad Pet to Good Pet and the target is near the center. The Psychic says goldfish. The team immediately debates whether goldfish are good or bad pets and anchors heavily toward one extreme. They set the dial far toward Good Pet. But the clue was meant to communicate middle of the spectrum, not an extreme. The team over-interpreted the clue and missed the target entirely.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Think About Relative Position, Not Just the Clue
walkthrough.text: When setting the dial, think about where the clue falls relative to both endpoints. Do not just react to whether the clue feels positive or negative. Ask yourself: on this specific spectrum, how far from center does this clue land? A goldfish on the Bad Pet to Good Pet spectrum is decidedly middle. It is not a cockroach and it is not a golden retriever. Place the dial based on relative distance between the extremes.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have just experienced the core Wavelength experience. You learned that clean, one-thought clues work best, the Psychic must maintain total silence after giving their clue, and teams should think about relative spectrum position rather than anchoring to extremes. The joy of Wavelength is discovering how your teammates think and calibrating your shared wavelength over many rounds.

walkthrough.title: Tune In
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your own game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategies on clue-giving and dial-setting.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to scoring, clue rules, and special situations.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Get on the same wavelength!

Team Moon's Left guess was wrong → 0 pts

Teammates adjust based on reaction — breaks the rules

Poker face is part of being a good Psychic

Joy of the game: calibrating your shared wavelength

summary.title: Tune In
bullets: Check out General Tips → clue-giving and dial-setting strategies
Reference the Appendix → scoring, clue rules, special situations
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Anchor Both Endpoints First
walkthrough.text: Before placing the dial, quickly identify what the extreme ends of the spectrum would look like. If the spectrum is Scary to Not Scary, think about what is the scariest thing imaginable and what is the least scary. Then ask where the Psychic's clue falls between those two anchors. This calibration step takes five seconds and dramatically improves accuracy. Without anchors, your dial placement is just a gut reaction.

walkthrough.title: Give Concrete, Specific Clues
walkthrough.text: As the Psychic, concrete nouns and proper names work much better than abstract descriptions. Instead of saying something pretty fast for a Slow to Fast spectrum, say cheetah or bullet train. Specific references give your teammates a shared mental model to work from. Vague or abstract clues force your team to guess what you mean, adding unnecessary uncertainty to an already tricky task.

walkthrough.title: Avoid Soft Modifiers
walkthrough.text: Words like very, kind of, somewhat, and almost are tempting but unhelpful. If the target is near one end, do not say something very hot. Instead, pick a clue that inherently communicates that intensity. Lava is inherently near-maximum heat without needing very in front of it. Let the clue itself carry the positioning information rather than adding weak modifiers that everyone interprets differently.

walkthrough.title: Know Your Teammates
walkthrough.text: Wavelength rewards shared cultural context. If your teammates all love sports, use sports references. If your team watches the same movies, use film references. The best clue is one that your specific teammates will interpret the same way you do. Over the course of a game, you will learn how your teammates think and calibrate your clues accordingly. This is the heart of the Wavelength experience.

walkthrough.title: Left/Right Guessing Strategy
walkthrough.text: When guessing Left or Right as the opposing team, think about the Psychic's intent rather than your own interpretation. The question is not where do I think this clue falls, but where does the Psychic think this clue falls and did their team overshoot or undershoot? Model the Psychic's likely target, then compare it to the dial position. This meta-thinking gives you better odds than a personal gut reaction.

walkthrough.title: Rotate Psychics Often
walkthrough.text: Different players bring different clue-giving styles and strengths. Rotate the Psychic role every round to give everyone a chance to give clues and to prevent one player's style from becoming predictable. Fresh perspectives keep the game exciting and help the team learn how each member thinks. Variety in Psychics also makes it harder for the opposing team to read your patterns.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Here are the biggest Wavelength mistakes in one place. Multi-part clues that confuse instead of clarifying. Psychic reacting to discussion through facial expressions or body language. Using numbers or percentages as dial shorthand, which bypasses the intended social inference. Anchoring the dial to one extreme without considering relative position. Overthinking the Left/Right guess instead of making a quick directional call. And giving clues that use words from the spectrum card itself, which is not allowed.

walkthrough.title: Keep the Energy Up
walkthrough.text: Wavelength is a party game and the best games have enthusiastic, engaged players. Celebrate great clues and big scores. Laugh when a clue totally misses. Do not take wrong guesses personally. The reveal moment is the highlight of every round, so build anticipation before opening the screen. A fun, energetic table makes better clues and better guesses than a quiet, analytical one.

summary.title: Anchor Both Endpoints First
bullets: Identify extreme examples for each end of the spectrum
Place clue between those anchors — 5 seconds improves accuracy dramatically

bullets: Concrete nouns and proper names > abstract descriptions
'Cheetah' > 'something pretty fast'

summary.title: Avoid Soft Modifiers
bullets: Skip 'very,' 'kind of,' 'somewhat' — everyone interprets differently
Let the clue itself carry the positioning info

summary.title: Know Your Teammates
bullets: Use shared cultural references your team will interpret the same way
Calibrate clue style to your specific teammates

summary.title: Left/Right Guessing Strategy
bullets: Think about the Psychic's intent, not your own interpretation
Model: where did the Psychic aim → did the team over/undershoot?

summary.title: Rotate Psychics Often
bullets: Fresh perspectives keep the game exciting
Variety makes patterns harder for opponents to read

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Multi-part clues, Psychic body language leaks
Using numbers/percentages, extreme anchoring bias
Clues using words from the spectrum card (not allowed)

summary.title: Keep the Energy Up
bullets: Celebrate great clues, laugh at misses
Build anticipation before the reveal — it's the highlight

walkthrough.title: Precision Clues vs Robust Clues
walkthrough.text: A precision clue targets the exact center of the target zone for maximum four-point potential, but if your team misinterprets it slightly, you score zero. A robust clue is one that most people will place in roughly the right area, scoring two or three points reliably. Advanced Psychics choose between precision and robustness based on the target position and their confidence in the team's interpretation.

walkthrough.title: Edge and Center Strategies
walkthrough.text: Targets near the extreme ends of the spectrum are easier to clue because extreme examples are obvious. Targets near the center are harder because middle-ground clues are inherently ambiguous. For center targets, pick a clue that is clearly neither extreme. For edge targets, pick something that is obviously close to the endpoint. The difficulty of each round depends heavily on where the target lands.

walkthrough.title: Card Side Selection
walkthrough.text: When you draw a Wavelength card, you choose which of the two sides to use. Advanced Psychics consider the target position before choosing. If the target is near one extreme, look for a spectrum side where that extreme has a clear, universally understood anchor. If the target is in the middle, choose a spectrum where middle-ground examples are easy to think of. The right spectrum makes a hard target easy.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Opposing Team
walkthrough.text: When making your Left/Right guess, watch the Psychic's team discussion carefully. If the team debated heavily before settling on a position, they were likely uncertain, which means the target could be far from where they placed the dial. If the team quickly agreed, the clue was probably clear and the dial is close to the target. Use the team's confidence level as information for your binary guess.

walkthrough.title: The Catch-Up Rule
walkthrough.text: If the active team scores four points in a round and is still losing, they get an immediate extra turn. This catch-up mechanic means falling behind is not hopeless. Advanced teams will take bigger risks when behind, aiming for the four-point center zone, because the potential catch-up bonus compounds the reward. When ahead, play more conservatively and aim for reliable two or three-point scores.

walkthrough.title: Denying the Center
walkthrough.text: When the active team hits the four-point center zone, the opposing team scores zero from their Left/Right guess regardless of accuracy. This means the opposing team should root for the active team to miss the center. As the active team, hitting center is the best possible outcome because it maximizes your score and denies the opponent their bonus point simultaneously. Always aim for center when feasible.

walkthrough.title: Cooperative Mode for Smaller Groups
walkthrough.text: With two to five players, the cooperative mode is an excellent way to play. Start with seven random cards in a special slot. Everyone works together as one team trying to maximize points. The deck runs out and you compare your final score against a target. Center hits are worth three points instead of four but grant a bonus card that extends the game. Cooperative mode emphasizes group calibration without the competitive pressure.

walkthrough.title: Building Team Wavelength Over Time
walkthrough.text: The more you play Wavelength with the same group, the better you get at reading each other. You learn that one teammate always interprets clues conservatively while another goes extreme. You discover which cultural references land and which confuse. Over many games, your team develops a shared vocabulary and calibration that makes you devastatingly accurate. This long-term improvement is what makes Wavelength a game people come back to again and again.

summary.title: Precision Clues vs Robust Clues
bullets: Precision: targets center zone (4 pts) but risky if misread
Robust: reliably lands in target area (2-3 pts) but lower ceiling

summary.title: Edge and Center Strategies
bullets: Extreme targets = easier (obvious anchor examples)
Center targets = harder (middle-ground clues are ambiguous)

summary.title: Card Side Selection
bullets: Choose spectrum side that makes your target position easiest to clue
Right spectrum turns a hard target into an easy one

summary.title: Reading the Opposing Team
bullets: Heavy debate = uncertain → target may be far from dial
Quick agreement = clear clue → dial is likely close

summary.title: The Catch-Up Rule
bullets: Score 4 pts and still losing → extra turn (catch-up bonus)
Behind: take big risks for center. Ahead: play conservatively

summary.title: Denying the Center
bullets: Center hit = opponent gets 0 from Left/Right guess
Maximizes your score AND denies bonus — always aim center when feasible

summary.title: Cooperative Mode for Smaller Groups
bullets: 2-5 players: work as 1 team, 7-card deck, compare final score
Center = 3 pts + bonus card extending the game

summary.title: Building Team Wavelength Over Time
bullets: Repeat plays build shared vocabulary and calibration
Long-term improvement makes you devastatingly accurate

entries.definition: The active team's clue giver for the round. Sees the hidden target, gives one clue, then must remain completely silent until the round ends. Rotates each round.
Physical game component with a spinning wheel, hidden target, blue screen to hide/reveal the target, and a red dial that teams turn to make their guess.
Double-sided card showing two opposite concepts (e.g., Hot/Cold, Good/Bad). The Psychic draws one and chooses a side. The two endpoints define the spectrum for that round.
A colored zone on the wheel that is hidden behind the blue screen. Randomly positioned by spinning the wheel. Contains 3 scoring zones: center (4 pts), adjacent (3 pts and 2 pts).
The pointer that the active team's teammates rotate to indicate where they think the hidden target is on the spectrum. Locked in before the reveal.
Cover on the Wavelength device that hides the target. The Psychic opens it to see the target, then closes it. Opened again at scoring to reveal the result.
After the active team locks the dial, the opposing team guesses whether the actual target is to the left or right of the dial position. Correct guess = 1 bonus point (0 if center hit).
The innermost scoring zone of the hidden target. Active team scores 4 points and denies the opposing team any Left/Right bonus. The best possible outcome.
Scoring zones flanking the center. Active team scores 3 or 2 points depending on which zone the dial lands in. Edge ties go to the active team.
If the active team scores 4 points and is still behind, they get an immediate extra turn with a different Psychic. Prevents runaway leads.

One thought only. No combining ideas. Clue must reference something real or established fictional. No words from the card. No numbers, percentages, or numeric hints. Must relate to the spectrum concepts.
After giving the clue, the Psychic cannot speak, gesture, make facial expressions, or communicate in any way until the round ends. Neutral expression required.
Alternative mode for 2-12 players (best at 2-5). One team, 7-card deck. Center zone = 3 pts + bonus card. No Left/Right phase. Score against a target when deck runs out.
Tiebreaker procedure. Each team takes one turn. Compare round scores including Left/Right bonus. Repeat until one team wins a round outright.
Game ends when any team reaches 10 points. Higher score wins. If tied, sudden-death rounds resolve the winner.
